command: Mount-DiskImage -ImagePath "C:\Temp\archive.iso"; $iso=(Get-DiskImage "C:\Temp\archive.iso" | Get-Volume).DriveLetter; $usb=(Get-Volume | Where-Object {$_.DriveType -eq 'Removable'} | Select-Object -First 1 -ExpandProperty DriveLetter); Copy-Item "$iso:\" "$usb:\Backup" -Recurse -Force; Dismount-DiskImage -ImagePath "C:\Temp\archive.iso"
explanation: Mounts a local ISO, copies its contents to a USB drive, and dismounts, populating the removable media with exfil content via a local mount.
rationale_steps: Mount a local disk image to access packaged data.
Determine both ISO and USB drive letters.
Copy all ISO contents to the USB and then dismount.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: Well-formed PowerShell with balanced quotes/braces, valid cmdlets and separators, and plausible parameter usage throughout.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: It mounts an ISO, finds a removable drive, copies contents to it, and dismounts—consistent with T1052—though success may hinge on environment details like Get-DiskImage|Get-Volume behavior and the destination folder existing.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is well-formed with balanced quotes and parentheses, valid PowerShell cmdlets chained with semicolons, though the Copy-Item path construction using string interpolation with drive letters (e.g., "$iso:\" ) may have minor edge-case issues with path formatting.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command correctly implements T1052 by mounting an ISO containing staged exfiltration content, identifying a removable USB drive dynamically, copying the contents recursively, and dismounting — closely matching the technique's staging-and-transfer pattern, with only a minor realism gap in that Copy-Item's source path "$iso:\" may need adjustment to "${iso}:\" for reliable variable expansion.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command uses proper PowerShell syntax, correct cmdlet names, valid piping, and correctly balanced quotes and variables.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The logic correctly identifies the drive letters for both the mounted ISO and the removable media to perform the file transfer as described.
